questions.questionText: The most common cause of cirrhosis worldwide is:
questions.optionB: Chronic hepatitis B and C, alcoholic liver disease
questions.explanation: Cirrhosis: end-stage chronic liver disease. Irreversible fibrosis with regenerative nodules and disrupted architecture. Causes: HCV (most common in developed countries), HBV (most common worldwide), alcohol (2nd most common in Western countries), NAFLD/NASH (rising), autoimmune, PBC, PSC, hemochromatosis, Wilson's. Complications: portal hypertension, ascites, varices, hepatic encephalopathy, HCC, coagulopathy. Child-Pugh score for severity. Reference: Robbins — Cirrhosis.

questions.questionText: Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Autoimmune destruction of small intrahepatic bile ducts, anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA)
questions.explanation: PBC: autoimmune, chronic progressive cholestatic disease. Destruction of small/medium intrahepatic bile ducts → cholestasis → secondary biliary cirrhosis. AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibody): positive in ~95% (anti-pyruvate dehydrogenase E2 subunit — highly specific). Elevated ALP. Florid duct lesion: granulomatous inflammation around bile ducts. Middle-aged women (F:M = 9:1). Pruritus, fatigue, xanthomas, jaundice (late). Treatment: ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA). Reference: Robbins — PBC.

questions.questionText: Hepatitis B virus is associated with which type of hepatocellular change?
questions.optionB: Ground-glass hepatocytes (accumulation of HBsAg in smooth ER)
questions.explanation: HBV: ground-glass hepatocytes — pale, finely granular eosinophilic cytoplasm due to accumulation of HBsAg in dilated smooth endoplasmic reticulum. Characteristic histological finding. Confirmed by orcein stain or immunohistochemistry for HBsAg. HBV is a DNA virus (only DNA hepatitis virus). Can integrate into host genome → cancer without cirrhosis. Serology: HBsAg (active infection/carrier), anti-HBs (immunity), HBeAg (high infectivity), anti-HBc IgM (acute). Reference: Robbins — HBV.

questions.questionText: Cholelithiasis (gallstones) — the most common type of gallstones in developed countries is:
questions.explanation: Gallstones: cholesterol stones (~80% in Western countries) vs. pigment stones (black — hemolytic anemias, cirrhosis; brown — biliary infections, parasites). Cholesterol stone risk factors (4 Fs + more): Female, Fat (obese), Fertile (multiparous), Forty, plus estrogen therapy, rapid weight loss, Native American, fibrates. Complications: biliary colic, acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, cholangitis, gallstone ileus, porcelain gallbladder (calcified wall → cancer risk). Reference: Robbins — Gallstones.

questions.optionB: Hepatic vein thrombosis (or obstruction of hepatic venous outflow)
questions.explanation: Budd-Chiari syndrome: obstruction of hepatic venous outflow (hepatic vein thrombosis or IVC obstruction in the hepatic segment). Causes: hypercoagulable states (polycythemia vera — most common association, PNH, OCP, myeloproliferative disorders, Factor V Leiden), hepatic tumors (HCC invasion). Presentation: painful hepatomegaly, ascites, jaundice (triad). Liver: centrilobular congestion and necrosis ('nutmeg liver' pattern). Diagnosis: Doppler US, CT/MRI. Treatment: anticoagulation, TIPS, liver transplant. Reference: Robbins — Budd-Chiari.

questions.questionText: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is strongly associated with:
questions.optionB: Ulcerative colitis (UC) and has 'onion-skin' periductal fibrosis of large bile ducts
questions.explanation: PSC: chronic cholestatic liver disease with fibrosis and stricture of LARGE intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. 'Onion-skin' (periductal concentric fibrosis). ERCP: 'beading' pattern (alternating strictures and dilations). Strongly associated with UC (~70% have UC, but only ~5% of UC develop PSC). p-ANCA may be positive. Increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma (10-15% lifetime risk). AMA negative (unlike PBC). Treatment: liver transplant for advanced disease. Reference: Robbins — PSC.

questions.questionText: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is most commonly associated with:
questions.optionB: Chronic hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis of any etiology
questions.explanation: HCC: most common primary liver malignancy. Risk factors: HBV (most common cause worldwide, can cause HCC WITHOUT cirrhosis — DNA integrates), HCV, cirrhosis (any etiology — alcohol, NASH, hemochromatosis, α1AT deficiency), aflatoxin B1 (Aspergillus flavus — TP53 mutation R249S). AFP elevated. Can invade hepatic vein/IVC. Single mass or multifocal. Green color on cut section (bile production). Fibrolamellar variant: young, non-cirrhotic, better prognosis, normal AFP. Reference: Robbins — HCC.

questions.questionText: Alcoholic liver disease progresses through which stages?
questions.explanation: ALD progression: (1) Hepatic steatosis (fatty liver): earliest, reversible, macrovesicular fat. (2) Alcoholic hepatitis: Mallory-Denk bodies (damaged cytokeratin intermediate filaments), neutrophilic infiltration, hepatocyte ballooning/necrosis, fever, jaundice, RUQ pain, AST/ALT elevated (AST:ALT >2:1 typical). (3) Cirrhosis: micronodular initially → macronodular. Alcohol metabolism: ADH (cytosol) and CYP2E1 (microsomes) → acetaldehyde → ALDH → acetate. Increased NADH/NAD+ ratio. Reference: Robbins — ALD.
